AAT 9821948
DEVELOPMENT AND APPLICATION OF A GENETICALLY-STRUCTURED SIMULATION FOR BACTERIOPHAGE T7 (BIOINFORMATICS, ANTISENSE RNA, DRUG STRATEGIES)
ENDY, ANDREW DAVID
DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
PHD
1997
YIN, JOHN
DAI-B 59/01, p. 1, Jul 1998
188
ENGINEERING, CHEMICAL (0542); BIOLOGY, MOLECULAR (0307); BIOPHYSICS, GENERAL (0786); HEALTH SCIENCES, PHARMACOLOGY (0419)
 5.80Mb image-only PDF
click here for UMI dissertations
A genetically structured simulation for the complete intracellular growth of bacteriophage T7 (a virus of the bacteria E. coli) is developed by combining engineering principles with mechanistic and kinetic information from molecular biology and genetics. The resulting model is evaluated against experimental data, and is shown to produce a reasonable representation of the viral growth process, from DNA entry and gene expression to viral particle assembly. Gene expression is modeled on an individual gene basis. The base model is recast in a general form to allow the simulation of any ordering of the 122 genetic elements of T7 (genes, promoters, terminators, RNase sites, and blank regions). Using these models as tools, we have begun to develop an understanding of how biological systems efficiently encode, express and maintain their genetic information. This knowledge is applied to search for and develop effective antiviral strategies (e.g., antisense RNA) capable of resisting viral evolution, and to design, search, and select alternative genome structures and substructures satisfying specific objective functions (e.g., a predetermined viral growth rate).
